1.pericope_label: Hallelujah! Heaven's Praise at Babylon's Fall
1.exposition: The scene turns from earth's smoking ruin to heaven's exultation. Four times in five verses the word “Hallelujah” rings out — the Hebrew shout of the Psalms, and, remarkably, its only appearances in the New Testament. A great multitude begins, ascribing salvation, glory, honour, and power to God because his judgments are true and righteous: he has judged the great harlot and avenged the blood of his servants (v.2). A second Hallelujah answers as Babylon's smoke rises forever (v.3); the twenty-four elders and four living creatures fall down with “Amen, Hallelujah” (v.4); and a voice from the throne summons all God's servants, small and great, to praise (v.5). The very judgment the merchants of earth had just mourned is, in heaven, the occasion of worship. Justice finally done is a thing to sing about.
1.original_language: “Hallelujah” (hallĕlû-Yah, “praise Yah”) is the liturgical cry that frames the Egyptian Hallel (Psalms 113–118); its fourfold repetition here is the only place it surfaces in the New Testament. The doxology's four nouns — sōtēria (salvation), doxa (glory), timē (honour), dynamis (power) — form one complete ascription. “Amen” (v.4) is the assembly's word of assent, “so be it, it is true”; joined to “Hallelujah” it is the fullest possible response of the worshiping community — and latin-fathers Bede notes the Church keeps both words in their original Hebrew out of reverence for their sacredness.
1.historical_context: The Hallel (Psalms 113–118) was the Passover praise — the very hymn Jesus and the disciples sang before Gethsemane (Matthew 26:30; Mark 14:26). Heaven's Hallelujah over fallen Babylon is thus a Passover shout, the cry of redemption from the last great Egypt. “The blood of his servants” avenged (v.2) was no abstraction: the churches of Asia knew real martyrs under Roman persecution, whose cry “how long?” (6:10) is now answered. And the smoke rising forever (v.3) echoes God's perpetual judgment on Edom in Isaiah 34:10 — irreversibility as a mark of divine justice.
1.christ: The servants whose blood is avenged (v.2) are those who held “the testimony of Jesus” (v.10); their vindication is inseparable from the Lamb's own. The Hallel that once celebrated the exodus from Egypt now celebrates the greater exodus secured by the Lamb who was slain (Revelation 5:9). The worship that began around the throne in Revelation 4–5, where the Lamb alone was found worthy to open the scroll, now fills heaven as his worthiness is proved out in history.

verses.note: After the dirges of chapter 18, a great voice of much people in heaven cries “Hallelujah.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear it as heaven's direct answer to the summons of 18:20 — “rejoice over her” — the redeemed ascribing salvation, glory, honour, and power to God; this is the first of the chapter's four Hallelujahs, the word's only occurrences in the New Testament. latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede read the church triumphant rejoicing over the fall of the great city.

The ground of the praise: true and righteous are his judgments. grammatical-historical Barnes and Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note the refrain already heard at 15:3 and 16:7 — God's judgments are faithful and just. Two grounds are named: he has judged the harlot who corrupted the earth, and avenged the blood of his servants at her hand (cf. 18:24). latin-fathers Bede sees the harlot's twin crimes — she corrupted the good and persecuted them — and in these two all the crimes of the wicked comprehended.

Again they said, Hallelujah — and her smoke rose up forever and ever. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the second shout intensifies the celebration; the perpetual smoke, drawn from the desolation of Edom in Isaiah 34:10, seals the permanence of her ruin. latin-fathers Bede fastens on the tense: “rises up,” John says, not “will rise up” — Babylon is always going into perdition, in part already burning, while Jerusalem passes into paradise.

The twenty-four elders and the four living creatures fell down and worshipped God on the throne, sealing the praise with “Amen; Hallelujah.” grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott mark the adoration of the representatives of the redeemed and the heavenly host, last seen bowing at 11:16. latin-fathers Bede: the Church worships God “not with the service of the lips alone, but with the sweetness of the highest devotion,” keeping “Amen” and “Hallelujah” in their sacred Hebrew out of reverence.

A voice from the throne calls, “Praise our God, all ye his servants... both small and great.” grammatical-historical Barnes hears the summons that prepares for the great Hallelujah of the marriage (v.6). puritan Matthew Henry marks its reach: “small and great” embraces every rank of the faithful, none excluded — access to this praise runs not through wealth or station but through the fear of God.

witnesses.point: The fourfold doxology — salvation, glory, honour, power — ascribes all to God for his righteous judgment on Babylon; “Hallelujah” is the Hebrew praise-shout of the Psalms, here alone in the New Testament.
The Church extols the Lord for his judgments with unceasing love; her smoke “rises up” (present, not future), for Babylon is always going to perdition while Jerusalem passes to paradise.
The throne's summons — “praise our God... both small and great” — embraces the whole company of the faithful, of every rank and degree, none excluded.

external.name: Robert H. Mounce
external.point: The heavenly doxology can rejoice in the judgment precisely because it answers the martyrs' long cry — the fall of Babylon vindicates God's justice and shows him not indifferent to the blood of his saints.

1.application: Heaven does not avert its eyes from judgment; it sings. When evil is at last undone and the blood of the innocent answered, worship is not a distraction from justice but its fitting echo.

6.pericope_label: The Marriage Supper of the Lamb
6.exposition: The praise crests into the loudest sound in the book — a voice like a great multitude, like many waters, like mighty thunder — announcing that the Lord God omnipotent reigns (v.6). At once the vision turns from the fallen city to the waiting bride: the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his wife has made herself ready (v.7). She is granted fine linen, clean and white — the righteous deeds of the saints (v.8) — the deliberate opposite of the harlot's purple and scarlet. The fourth beatitude of Revelation blesses all who are called to the marriage supper (v.9), and John, overwhelmed, falls to worship the angel and is sharply corrected: worship God, for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy (v.10). Revelation's two women — harlot and bride — and its two cities now stand side by side; and heaven's deepest joy is a wedding.
6.original_language: “Hallelujah, for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth” — the aorist ebasileusen (“reigned, has begun to reign”) marks not a new fact but the decisive demonstration of an eternal one: Babylon's fall proves that God reigns. The bride's linen is “granted” (edothē, “it was given her”) — grace precedes; yet the same linen is glossed as the dikaiōmata, the “righteous deeds,” of the saints. Both are held together in one garment: it is given, and it is woven of real obedience. At v.10 the angel's blunt imperative tọ̄ Theọ̄ proskynēson, “worship God!”, is the book's whole theology of worship in two words.
6.historical_context: The messianic banquet was deep in Jewish hope — the LORD's feast of rich food on Mount Zion in Isaiah 25:6–8 — and Jesus made the wedding his own parable (Matthew 22:2; Luke 14:15–16). White linen was the priestly vestment (Exodus 28:39), and Zechariah's high priest reclothed in clean garments (Zechariah 3:3–5) is its Old Testament prototype. Angel-worship was a live danger in the province, warned against at Colossians 2:18; the angel's refusal here — “worship God!” — is the book's monotheistic refrain in miniature, set against a world commanded to worship the emperor.
6.christ: The Lamb is here the Bridegroom whose wedding has come. Scripture had long cast God as Israel's husband (Hosea; Ezekiel 16), and Isaiah 62:5 sang, “as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so shall your God rejoice over you.” Christ inherits that bridal love as the one who “loved the church and gave himself up for her... to present her to himself in splendour” (Ephesians 5:25–27). The Lamb who was slain (Revelation 5:6) now keeps the wedding his own blood purchased. And “the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy” (v.10) binds all true prophecy to a single content: witness to him.

verses.note: The voice of a great multitude, as of many waters and mighty thunderings, cries “Hallelujah” — the fourth and climactic. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the majesty of the chorus, loud as sea and storm (cf. 14:2), and its ground: “the Lord God omnipotent reigneth.” The Almighty has taken his power and reigns (11:17); the fall of Babylon is itself the proof that God's kingdom stands unhindered.

The marriage of the Lamb is come, and his wife hath made herself ready. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the consummated union of Christ and the church (cf. Ephesians 5:25–32; Matthew 22:2) — the long-awaited wedding of the Lamb with his bride, the cause of heaven's joy. Her very readiness is a fruit of grace, unfolded in the next verse.

To her was granted to be arrayed in fine linen, clean and white — and John interprets it: the fine linen is the righteousness of the saints. grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the pointed contrast with the harlot's purple and scarlet (17:4); reformed Gill weighs the two readings — the saints' righteous deeds (the visible holiness of the redeemed), or, more deeply, the righteousness of Christ worked in them — but presses the word “granted”: the wedding-dress is a gift.

The fourth beatitude: Blessed are they which are called unto the marriage supper of the Lamb. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the guests are the redeemed, invited to the feast of the consummated union (cf. Luke 14:15–16). The angel adds the seal, “these are the true sayings of God” — the authentication against which no imperial counter-claim can stand.

Overwhelmed, John fell at his feet to worship him, and the angel recoils: “see thou do it not... worship God.” grammatical-historical Robertson notes the angel names himself a fellow-servant of all who hold the testimony of Jesus (so again at 22:8–9) — worship belongs to God alone. Then the great saying: “the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy” — the animating heart of all prophecy is to bear witness to Christ.

witnesses.point: The marriage of the Lamb is the consummated union of Christ and the church; the bride “made herself ready,” and her readiness is the cause of heaven's joy.
The fine linen is the righteousness of the saints, granted by grace — whether their righteous deeds or the righteousness of Christ worked in them, the word “granted” makes it a gift.
The angel refuses John's worship as a fellow-servant — “worship God” — and declares that the testimony of Jesus is the very spirit of prophecy.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: The bride's linen is at once “given” and “the righteous deeds of the saints,” so grace and faithful obedience are not rivals but one fabric — the bride is clothed by gift and dressed in a life.

6.application: The harlot bought her adornment; the bride is given hers. What clothes you before God is never your own purchase but always a grant of grace — received, and then worn out in a life of real obedience.

11.pericope_label: The Rider on the White Horse
11.exposition: Now heaven itself opens, and the fullest vision of Christ since chapter 1 rides out. On a white horse sits one called Faithful and True, who judges and wages war in righteousness (v.11); his eyes are flame, his head bears many diadems, and he wears a name no one knows but himself (v.12); his robe is dipped in blood, and his name is the Word of God (v.13). The armies of heaven follow — on white horses, clothed in the bride's own fine linen, bearing no weapons (v.14). For the only weapon is the sharp sword of his mouth, with which he smites the nations, rules them with a rod of iron, and treads the winepress of God's wrath (v.15). And on his robe and thigh is the name that gathers up every claim to sovereignty: KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS (v.16). This is no contest between equals; it is the appearing of the King before whom the beast's whole coalition cannot stand.
11.original_language: The rider's titles mount in a deliberate cascade. He is “Faithful and True” (pistos kai alēthinos), the self-designation of the Christ who addressed Laodicea (3:14). His crowns are diadēmata — royal crowns, not the victor's wreath — and many of them, surpassing the seven of the dragon (12:3) and the ten of the beast (13:1). His name is “The Word of God” (ὁ Lógos toῦ Theoῦ), the Johannine title of the Prologue (John 1:1). The “sharp sword” (rhomphaia) proceeds from his mouth — the word, not steel. And the climactic inscription, basileus basileōn kai kyrios kyriōn, “King of kings and Lord of lords,” absorbs and supersedes every earthly title.
11.historical_context: The imagery is drenched in the divine-warrior tradition. The blood-dipped robe comes from Isaiah 63:1–3, where God returns from Edom with garments stained red — “I have trodden the winepress alone.” The sword of the mouth and the rod of iron draw on Isaiah 11:4 and Psalm 2:9; the winepress on Isaiah 63:3. “King of kings” was the Persian imperial title (applied to Artaxerxes at Ezra 7:12, to Nebuchadnezzar at Ezekiel 26:7); Domitian preferred to be hailed Dominus et Deus, “Lord and God.” Rome staged the returning general's triumph as a parade of conquest — but John shows the true Triumphator, whose robe is already red before the fighting begins, his name worn at the sword-thigh where those who follow can read it.
11.christ: Here Christ is disclosed most fully as the returning King. He is “The Word of God” (v.13) — the same creative Word of John 1:1 by whom all things were made, now coming to execute judgment. He is “Faithful and True,” so that his war, unlike every earthly conquest, is waged in righteousness (v.11). The robe is bloodied before a single blow is struck: the witnesses divide — chiefly the blood of his enemies from Isaiah 63, yet the Lamb who was slain (Revelation 5:6) may wear his own atoning blood, the judge arriving marked by the death that grounds his right to judge (John 5:27). The many diadems declare that all the authority the dragon grasped after is his; the secret name, that his being runs deeper than any creature can sound; the title on the thigh, that the crucified Lamb is the universal King.

verses.note: I saw heaven opened, and behold a white horse; and its rider is called Faithful and True. latin-fathers Victorinus of Pettau sees “the horse, and He that sits upon him,” setting forth “our Lord coming to His kingdom with the heavenly army,” the nations gathered to judgment. grammatical-historical Barnes marks the title “Faithful and True” (from 3:14) and the white horse of the conqueror (6:2); unlike earthly warlords he “in righteousness doth judge and make war.” reformed Gill hears Christ coming forth in the latter days through the advance of the gospel and the overthrow of antichrist — the historicist reading set beside the futurist expectation of his glorious advent.

His eyes were as a flame of fire, and on his head were many crowns; and he had a name written that no man knew but himself. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the flaming eyes of his searching omniscience (as at 1:14) and the many diadems of his manifold sovereignty — many crowns, for he is King of kings. The secret name marks a depth of his being beyond all human knowledge, an unsearchable and transcendent majesty.

He was clothed with a vesture dipped in blood: and his name is called The Word of God. Interpreters divide over the blood. reformed Gill and wesleyan-arminian Wesley take it chiefly as the blood of his enemies, slain in judgment — the winepress-treader of Isaiah 63:1–3 whose garments are stained red — though Gill notes the older reading of his own atoning blood. The name settles his identity: “The Word of God,” the eternal Logos of John 1:1, now coming in judgment.

The armies which were in heaven followed him upon white horses, clothed in fine linen, white and clean. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note they wear the bride's own array (v.8, the righteousness of the saints) and ride the conqueror's white — saints and angels sharing his triumph, yet bearing no weapons: the victory will be his alone.

Out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations; he rules them with a rod of iron and treads the winepress of God's wrath. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson stress that the sword is the word of his judgment (as 1:16; Isaiah 11:4; 2 Thessalonians 2:8) — he conquers not by carnal weapons but by the word of his power. eastern Cyril of Jerusalem hears in the “rod of iron” (Psalm 2:9) the stone cut without hands of Daniel's vision, Christ's kingdom breaking and overpowering the empires of men.

On his vesture and on his thigh a name written, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the title of absolute supremacy, already his at 17:14 (cf. 1 Timothy 6:15; Deuteronomy 10:17) — sovereign over every king and lord. reformed Gill marks the placement, on the robe and at the thigh where the sword hangs, conspicuous for all to read: the conquering Christ openly proclaimed the universal King.

witnesses.point: The white horse and its rider set forth our Lord coming to his kingdom with the heavenly army, gathering the souls of the nations to judgment.
The rider conquers by the sword of his mouth — the word of his power, not carnal weapons; his eyes flame, his crowns are many, and “Faithful and True” names his righteous war.
Reads the coming as Christ's triumphant advance through the gospel against antichrist, and the blood-dipped robe chiefly as the blood of his enemies slain in judgment.
The “rod of iron” of Psalm 2 is Daniel's stone cut without hands — Christ's kingdom overpowering and breaking the empires of the world.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The divine warrior's only weapon is the sword of his word, and his army rides in linen, not armour; because the robe is already soaked in blood before the battle, the blood is most naturally his own — the sacrifice by which he has already conquered.

11.application: The King rides to war, and his soldiers carry no swords. The church's share in his conquest is not force but faithful witness, worn like clean linen — for the decisive blood is already on his robe before the first blow falls.

17.exposition: One last invitation goes out — the grotesque twin of the wedding supper. An angel standing in the sun calls every bird of prey to gather to “the supper of the great God” (v.17), to feast on the flesh of kings and captains and mighty men, of all people, small and great (v.18). The beast and the kings of the earth muster their armies against the rider and his host (v.19) — and then, strikingly, there is no battle. The beast is captured, and with him the false prophet who deceived the world; the two are thrown alive into the lake of fire (v.20); and the rest are slain by the sword of his mouth, and the birds are gorged with their flesh (v.21). Where the marriage supper was the reward of faithful witness, this supper is the fate of those who made war on the witnesses. What rose from the sea in chapter 13 is cast down; the issue was never in doubt.
17.original_language: The angel's summons to “the supper (deipnon) of the great God” (v.17) is the deliberate anti-type of the “marriage supper (deipnon) of the Lamb” (v.9) — the same word, two suppers, opposite fates. The roll-call of the slain in v.18 — “free and bond, small and great” — sardonically recapitulates the whole Roman social order, and inverts the “small and great” summoned to praise at v.5: every rank the beast honoured is now equally food for birds. The beast and false prophet are “taken” (piazō, seized or arrested), not defeated in a contest of arms.
17.historical_context: The invitation to the birds is drawn straight from Ezekiel 39:17–20, God's summons to the carrion birds after the defeat of Gog — marking this the eschatological Gog-war. In the ancient world proper burial was essential to honour; to lie unburied, devoured by birds, was the ultimate disgrace. Rome routinely left crucified bodies for the birds and dogs (Jewish law required burial by nightfall, Deuteronomy 21:22–23). The reversal is exact: those who denied the witnesses a grave are themselves left for the birds. And where a Roman triumph ended in feasting, this triumph ends in a feast upon the vanquished.
17.christ: The rider's victory here fulfils the promises made to the churches — to Thyatira, that he would rule the nations with a rod of iron (Revelation 2:27, quoting Psalm 2:9); to Smyrna, the crown of life to the faithful unto death (Revelation 2:10). The beast granted authority “over every tribe and people” (Revelation 13:7) is cast alive into the lake of fire (v.20); the persecutor's apparent invincibility ends. And Christ conquers, to the last, by “the sword of his mouth” (v.21) — the word of the Word of God. No army wins this battle; the one who was slain speaks, and the war is over.

verses.note: An angel standing in the sun cries to all the birds of heaven: “come and gather yourselves unto the supper of the great God.” grammatical-historical Barnes hears the grim, ironic feast — the birds summoned to feed on the slain, drawn from Ezekiel 39:17–20. reformed Gill marks the herald “in the sun,” conspicuous and far-heard, announcing the coming slaughter — “the supper of the great God” set in dreadful contrast to the marriage supper of the Lamb (v.9).

That ye may eat the flesh of kings, and captains, and mighty men... both small and great. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the total slaughter of the antichristian host, every rank embraced; reformed Gill, that no degree escapes — the whole confederacy, high and low, destroyed. The “small and great” summoned to feast on the fallen darkly answers the “small and great” summoned to praise God at v.5.

The beast, and the kings of the earth, and their armies, gathered together to make war against him that sat on the horse. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the final muster, the powers assembled as at Armageddon (16:14, 16); reformed Gill, the whole antichristian confederacy of the beast and the ten kings (17:14) presuming to war against Christ — a doomed and futile assault on the King of kings.

The beast was taken, and with him the false prophet — and these both were cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the two great powers are captured without a battle described, their overthrow swift and decisive; the false prophet's crime is his deception, the miracles by which he drew the world into beast-worship (13:13–14). reformed Gill marks this the first mention of the lake of fire (cf. 20:10) — the appointed end of the antichristian powers.

The remnant were slain with the sword of him that sat upon the horse, which sword proceeded out of his mouth, and all the fowls were filled with their flesh. grammatical-historical Robertson stresses once more that Christ conquers by the word of his mouth (v.15; 1:16), not by carnal weapons — the victory wholly his. puritan Matthew Henry: the grim feast is accomplished, the enemies of Christ utterly overthrown, and the vision closes before the binding of Satan.

witnesses.point: The beast and false prophet are simply “taken,” with no battle narrated; the armies fall to the sword of the rider's mouth — the word of his judgment, the victory wholly Christ's.
The beast and false prophet are cast alive into the lake of fire — the first mention of it — the decisive, final overthrow of the antichristian powers.
The “great supper of God” is the dreadful reverse of the marriage supper: where the saints are guests at the feast, the enemies of Christ are the food for birds.

external.name: Ian Paul
external.point: The “great supper of God” is the deliberate, ghastly antithesis of the Lamb's wedding supper — the same invitation-form, the same “small and great,” but at one feast the redeemed are guests and at the other the wicked are the meal.

17.application: Two suppers, and a single invitation to each. Revelation leaves no third option: to be a guest at the Lamb's table, or to be absent from it. The wedding and the war are finally the same event, seen from the two sides of the throne.

_meta.title: Hallelujah and the Rider — the King Returns
reflection: Heaven answers Babylon's fall not with silence but with four Hallelujahs (vv.1–6). What does it do to your picture of God that his justice is something the redeemed sing about?
The bride's wedding-dress is “granted” to her, yet it is “the righteousness of the saints” (v.8). How do you hold together the garment given by grace and the life woven into it?

The Rider wages war in righteousness, and his only weapon is the sword of his mouth (v.11, v.15). If the church shares his conquest dressed in linen, not armour, what does faithful witness look like where you are?
